prompt: Were there any issues from the meetings in the last couple days?

failures: expected at least one of [getMeetingsByDate, searchMeetingsByTopic] to fire — none did
score.finalText:
Yes. A few things came up that actually matter.

### What stands out from the last 2 days

- **Procurement and material shortages are still showing up in Uniqlo Phillipsburg NJ**
  - Mechanical tees were limited because of **credit card constraints** and cost control.
  - Missing guards and delayed pipe components were affecting field progress.
  - Air testing and riser work are moving, but there’s still clear dependency on material availability and cleanup of leaks.
  - What it means: this isn’t a catastrophe, but it’s a real execution drag. Small procurement friction is now touching schedule.

- **Process breakdowns around expense coding and approvals are creating reporting risk**
  - In the cost coding meeting, the team called out that **credit card transactions are being miscoded or submitted without proper job/cost info**, which is delaying billing and month-end reporting.
  - That’s not just admin noise. It slows financial visibility and makes it harder to trust project cost reporting in real time.
  - What it means: if PM oversight doesn’t tighten up, finance will keep chasing errors instead of closing books cleanly.

- **Vermillion Rise had contract-risk issues under active discussion**
  - The A141 discussion raised concerns around:
    - dispute timing,
    - subcontractor/supplier oversight,
    - vendor vetting and credit checks,
    - payment withholding timelines.
  - What it means: this is pre-execution risk management, which is good, but it also tells you the contract language still has enough ambiguity that it needs attention before it turns into a money fight later.

- **Goodwill jobs had a few live field issues**
  - **Tremont door threshold issue**
  - Fire sprinkler device still needs final scheduling/install before inspection
  - Brookville permit docs need resubmission after city comments
  - Roof leak investigation at Canton is still dependent on landlord action
  - What it means: mostly manageable jobsite issues, but they’re the kind that become owner-facing annoyances if they sit.

- **Union Collective has design coordination risk**
  - Layout decisions are still driving electrical/mechanical/lighting coordination.
  - Site slope and grading concerns were flagged as operational/maintenance issues.
  - What it means: still early enough to solve, but not early enough to stay vague. Owner decisions need to get locked.

### The real pattern

The pattern across the meetings is **not one major blow-up**. It’s a stack of smaller coordination failures:

- procurement friction
- approval delays
- coding/reporting sloppiness
- unresolved design details
- contract language still being negotiated

That’s how jobs get noisier before they get visibly off track.

### What I’d pay attention to first

1. **Uniqlo** — material flow and procurement controls  
   If the field team is rationing purchases because cards and ordering workflows are clunky, that needs to get cleaned up fast.

2. **Cost coding / approvals** — company-wide  
   This is operationally boring and financially dangerous. It directly affects billing speed and cost visibility.

3. **Vermillion Rise** — contract terms before execution  
   Better to fight over language now than claims later.

4. **Goodwill** — close the little owner-facing items quickly  
   Thresholds, permits, sprinkler device, roof leak coordination — none of these should linger.

### Bottom line

Yes — there were issues, but the bigger story is **friction**, not failure.  
Nothing here screams project collapse. A few of these absolutely could become avoidable schedule or financial problems if they sit for another week.

If you want, I can turn this into:
- a **tight leadership risk brief**, or
- a **project-by-project issue list with owners and next actions**.

data.message: Reading conversation memory and project context

output.title: Critical risks

recommendedNextActions: Open the meeting cards with risk or action badges first.
Convert verified ownership items into Tasks page records.
Use the linked meeting records as the evidence trail before sending an owner update.

decisions: 📏 Pipe Configuration: Main riser confirmed at 6 inches, supporting reduced head count while ensuring adequate flow and installation efficiency.
- **Pipe Configuration:** Main riser confirmed at 6 inches, supporting reduced head count while ensuring adequate flow and installation efficiency.
Key changes involved utilizing a new spacing approach and smaller pipes, confirmed by Chris Lopez and Kebba Mass, which would streamline installation and reduce labor costs by approximately 25%.

criticalRisks: - **Pioneer Parkway progress includes painting, plumbing, carpentry, and electrical work planned this week to advance completion** (00:04) - Painters will finish rooms and knee walls; plumbers install fixtures in the customer restroom - Carpenters focus on specialty installations and break room cabinetry - Electricians replace emergency lights, update sales floor fixtures, and wire the drinking fountain outlet - An added priority is installing an extra light near the front register as identified by Jared - **Washington site targets a substantial completion date of June 9, 2026, with flooring and painting scheduled to start soon** (02:18) - Flooring demo, grinding, and sealing will occur in offices, restrooms, and break rooms first - Painters will follow flooring completion to paint affected areas - Carpenters will frame openings for HVAC returns and build the cash wrap knee wall after register swaps - Plumbing installations include toilets, sinks, and rough-in for water fountains post-demo - **Canton site aims for substantial completion by June 30 with key structural works and leak investigations underway** (05:41) - Installation of 4x4 angle iron in the stock room and painting of sales floor and walls are active - Roof leaks are being investigated by the landlord for repair due to a longstanding issue - Carpentry work to demo support walls in the front soffit awaits final cost approval expected imminently - HVAC team will adjust thermostats above gondolas as part of return moves - **Site leadership praised ongoing communication and coordination, with no major concerns reported** (09:26) - Nick’s on-site management was commended for smooth interactions with stores - Scheduling of office moves and IT coordination remain critical to avoid disruption - Interns have started to assist Nick, increasing manpower for site coverage and support ## **Cost Management and Vendor Coordination** Cost control and vendor coordination remain high priorities with ongoing price evaluations and approvals.

- **HVAC return location costs were reviewed and reduced by adjusting return heights, approved by leadership** (04:12) - The cost-saving approach improves budget adherence without compromising functionality - All three subcontractors involved submitted pricing that informed the decision - **Electrical panel relocation decisions are paused pending budget review after incorporating upcoming store phases** (04:34) - Andrew Mulder will review overall budget after receiving estimates from the last four stores - Nick is actively managing site visits and pricing from subcontractors to support this assessment - **Pending approval of front soffit wall demolition costs expected shortly to proceed with structural changes** (06:44) - Allied’s proposal is under review and should be approved the same day as per Andrew - Approval enables timely carpentry work critical to next construction phases - **Landlord involvement in roof repairs reflects ongoing vendor coordination to resolve legacy maintenance issues** (07:02) - This external responsibility requires close communication to align on timelines and patching work - Nick will coordinate taping completion post-repair to finalize soffit area restoration ## **Operational and IT Coordination** The team emphasized the importance of synchronized office moves and IT support to minimize operational disruption.

- **Office moves at Washington will proceed with register swaps and phased IT support to maintain system access** (09:26) - Andrew confirmed approval of register rearrangements to create space for knee walls - Ian, the contracted IT specialist, is moving away, prompting a search for a new contact - Interim support may come from Dan Rit, with Andrew providing his contact for smooth communication - **Moving office equipment one room at a time helps maintain continuous computer access during transitions** (10:13) - This phased approach reduces risk of downtime or lost connectivity among store staff - Open communication channels between site and IT teams are critical for issue resolution - **Interns Victor and Hunter have started supporting site operations and will be introduced to site walkthroughs** (16:18) - They will gain hands-on experience under Douglas Franklin’s supervision at Allisonville and Noblesville - Their presence adds manpower to assist Nick and improve oversight across multiple projects ## **Project Closeout and Turnover Planning** Preparation for project turnovers includes detailed punch list management and staff training considerations.

meetings.title: Uniqlo & GPC Huddle
meetings.summary:
📏 Mechanical tees order limited: Limited mechanical tees order to 10 units to manage the $2,000+ invoice and credit card constraints, reducing expenses significantly.
💳 Credit card process clarified: Credit card increases must be requested by Brandon or Mar...
criticalRisks: Mechanical tees delayed, expected after lunch; Parker may pick up later.
Plan to pick up missing guards from Siva site to complete current site inventory.

- **Limited mechanical tees order to 10 units instead of 24** to manage the $2,000+ invoice and credit card constraints (00:07) - The original list included 24 tees, but only 10 will be purchased to reduce expense - Greg Davis explained the mechanical tees and couplings are needed for the riser installation - The mechanical tees are expected to arrive after 12 PM and may be picked up later by Parker or others - Cost per mechanical tee is about $33, making the total significant and prompting the cautious order size - **Credit card payment and limit increase process clarified** to avoid maxing out company cards (00:08) - Greg normally uses his card but it’s nearing its limit, causing concern - Kebba Mass explained credit card increases must be requested by Brandon or Maria by phone, not just via app - The team aims to avoid unnecessary payments on credit cards to control spending and avoid maxing out - Parker’s card was authorized for payment of $17+ to cover the current purchase, easing immediate cash flow - **Return and credit for unused supplies discussed** to recover costs on unused materials (00:34) - TJ Graves mentioned $460 spent on Windustrial fittings that were mostly unused due to project shifts - Kebba advised to return unused materials with receipts and note credits for proper accounting - This process helps reduce excess costs and maintain budget discipline - **Credit application submitted to negotiate better prices** for future purchases at Windustrial (00:33) - Greg noted that completing the credit application reduces prices for subsequent orders - Kebba committed to filling out and sending the application to secure lower rates - This strategic step supports cost savings on future material procurement ## **Project Execution and Site Updates** The team is progressing toward testing and final touches with clear plans for riser work and site transitions.
decisions: A decision was made to limit the mechanical tees order to 10 units due to high costs and credit card constraints.
## Notes ## **Materials Procurement and Budget Management** The team decided to limit the mechanical tees order due to high cost concerns and credit card limits.

- **Vertical pipes painting nearing completion; air compression testing imminent** at UNICO site (00:12) - TJ Graves reported one row of verticals left to paint before testing - The air compressor trailer just arrived, enabling air pressure tests to proceed - This milestone signals readiness to move into the punch list phase soon - **Riser work planned to start tomorrow barring leak repairs** with schedule aligned for next site moves (00:13) - TJ expects to begin riser installation the following day assuming leaks are fixed - Leak fixes are the only visible blocker ahead of riser work start - After finishing here, crews plan to move to GPC site by Thursday or Friday because of delays at CAA site - This sequencing aligns with material availability and racking delays, optimizing workforce use - **Pipe guards relocated to Siva site causing shortfall on current site** requiring retrieval (00:15) - TJ found that pipe guards were moved by Mike to Siva without prior notice - Kebba confirmed enough guards were ordered for both sites but retrieval from Siva is needed - The plan is to stop by Siva and bring the guards back to complete work on current site - This material movement caused a temporary shortage affecting installation progress - **Replacement vertical piece prepared and ready for lab delivery** with clear handoff plan (00:31) - TJ confirmed a replacement vertical pipe is ready to be sent out once approved - Kebba and TJ identified Parker as the likely person to deliver the sample to the lab - Documentation including the job start form with circled pipe locations has been provided to Parker - This ensures lab testing can proceed smoothly with proper traceability ## **Expense Coding and Financial Process Improvements** The team identified common credit card coding errors and agreed on a weekly review process to improve accuracy.
actionItems: Fill out and submit the credit application to Industrial to obtain lower pricing on future material purchases (33:40)
Coordinate weekly Friday morning sessions to review and correct credit card transactions with superintendents to improve coding accuracy (24:25)
Follow up with TJ Graves on hotel receipt coding and expense approvals to ensure timely processing (10:35)
Stay in touch with team for material pickup and project progress updates to maintain coordination (36:13)
Send updated material list and informal quote for mechanical tees and couplings to Kebba Mass for review (05:29)

meetings.title: Cost Coding and Approval CC Transactions & Time Sheets
meetings.summary:
📈 New Team Expansion: Five new hires added to handle projects valued at nearly $60 million, boosting current team from 4 to over 20.
💳 Improving Cost Coding: Instructional videos for new staff and increased PM oversight aimed at reducing billing delays and ...
criticalRisks: Manpower scheduling critical to balance current and upcoming jobs.

- **Current credit card transaction process causes billing delays and reporting roadblocks** as receipts often lack proper job codes or cost categories, making monthly reporting impossible until resolved (01:50) - Jesse plans to create instructional videos for new staff on correct coding procedures to reduce errors - Ashley will take responsibility for reviewing and verifying transactions to speed up approvals - The team acknowledged this task is critical to close out jobs and complete monthly financial reports - **PMs to increase involvement in weekly/monthly transaction reviews** to better control spending and coding accuracy, potentially during Friday weekly report meetings (18:30) - Kebba suggested a weekly review to catch common mistakes and improve consistency - Jesse emphasized the need for PMs to work closely with supers on coding before final approval to prevent errors and save time - **System visibility and permissions for transaction management explained** with Kebba showing dashboard views and filtering options to monitor all employee expenses (19:50) - Jesse demonstrated the ability to identify incorrect coding, missing receipts, and timing issues on charges - Kebba confirmed PMs can edit and correct cost coding to reduce administrative burden on Jesse - The goal is to empower field leadership to catch mistakes early, reducing the volume of required rework - **Awareness raised on spending habits and vendor costs** with Gregory investigating overcharges on car rentals to ensure compliance with quoted rates (17:30) - Jesse reminded the team to seek bargains and maintain cost-consciousness despite having spending freedoms - Monitoring and controlling discretionary costs is necessary to protect project budgets ## **Project Pipeline and Workload Management** The team is managing a robust pipeline of sprinkler projects and other builds with detailed scheduling and manpower alignment.

- **Copying senior PMs Doug and Bob on key emails is mandated to leverage their experience and maintain alignment** (00:55) - Jesse urged everyone to keep upper management informed on decisions and issues to benefit from their knowledge - This helps avoid miscommunication and keeps leadership in the loop for critical matters - **Monthly billing summaries to be shared with Andrew and Bryce to improve project financial oversight** (02:10) - Doug may review these summaries monthly with Andrew and Bryce to ensure transparency on project build values - This is intended to keep all stakeholders aware of revenue progress and job status - **Jesse plans to schedule next week’s meetings with a split focus between Doug’s and Bob’s teams** to streamline coordination across offices (00:55) - This will help reduce confusion for those working across both groups - Emphasizes clearer lines of communication and responsibility - **Team members encouraged to report manpower schedules and project statuses regularly** to help recruiting and PMs anticipate staffing needs (15:30) - Jesse emphasized using Teams messages or calls to update Jasmine’s spreadsheet promptly - This continuous communication loop supports proactive hiring and resource allocation ## **Productivity Culture and Workload Insights** The team is maintaining strong productivity with high workloads balanced by strategic hiring and process improvements.
decisions: Additionally, inclusion of senior PMs in key communications aims to enhance oversight and decision-making, while maintaining a positive office culture amidst growth.

## Shorthand Bullet 📧 **Communication Protocols** (01:00 - 01:30) Emphasis on copying senior PMs and upper management on project correspondence to keep them informed and leverage their knowledge for decision-making.

- **Adding five new team members to cover high-value projects** including Tony (PM starting Monday), Demetrius (assistant PM), Patrick Antonella (project engineer), and two interns, Victor and Hunter, who will assist Nick Bremmy and estimating respectively (08:30) - Tony will focus on the Exo and Vermillion projects, both valued near **$60 million** combined - Patrick has prior experience at FA Wilhelm, expected to bring valuable expertise, especially on large projects like XL Wilmer - Intern Victor supports Illinois projects, while Hunter helps in estimating to alleviate workload - Jesse emphasized the importance of quickly integrating these hires to maintain momentum on multiple concurrent projects - **Recruiting and manpower scheduling improvements underway** with Jasmine managing a dynamic spreadsheet to track project start and stop dates and manpower needs (14:50) - Kebba confirmed current coverage for ongoing projects including Utah and Florida, with potential hiring if new projects arise - Jesse stressed keeping updated manpower plans to avoid last-minute scrambling and overhiring risks - This system aims to balance staffing against workload to avoid firing or underutilization - **Experienced hires bring diverse backgrounds to strengthen team** with Tony’s GC business contacts expected to open new business opportunities in Indianapolis (07:30) - Jesse highlighted Tony’s semi-retired status but strong network as a strategic asset to convert leads into work - Patrick’s transition from teaching to construction management adds valuable project oversight skills for complex builds - **Team morale and office culture remain positive despite growth** with light-hearted banter and recognition of busy schedules, reflecting good camaraderie as team size grows from **4 to over 20** in recent years (05:50) - Jesse noted the pace and scale increase from **$10 million to $28 million** in revenue alongside team expansion - Andrew and Candon exchanged friendly comments, reinforcing a supportive work environment ## **Financial Processes and Cost Control** Improving credit card transaction oversight and cost coding accuracy is a priority to avoid billing delays and budget overruns.
actionItems: Copy senior PMs and upper management on all project correspondence to keep them informed and leverage their expertise for decision-making (01:00)
Collaborate with superintendents to properly code and approve credit card transactions
create training videos for new staff on this process (02:40)
Send updated list of jobs and billing amounts to Andrew and Bryce
coordinate monthly billing reviews with Doug (03:44)

meetings.title: Alleato QuickBooks transfer discussion
meetings.summary:
📈 Change in Accounting Software: Team shifted focus to QuickBooks, abandoning Acumatica due to complexity and poor support.
🔄 Simplified Retainage Handling: QuickBooks uses negative invoice line items for retainage, easing current pain points with Acumatica...
criticalRisks: - The test account approach is low risk and allows discovery of potential blockers without impacting live operations.
- This phased approach mitigates risk and allows adjustment based on pilot results.
[02:12] **Brandon Clymer**: Now as far as the reporting functions in some of the, some of the Ars and reports that QuickBooks can do, I concern me a little bit.
decisions: ## Short Summary The meeting centered on the decision to move from using Acumatica to QuickBooks as their primary accounting software.

## Notes ## **Accounting Software Strategy** The team decided to move away from pursuing a deal with **Acumatica** due to limited improvement opportunities and customer support issues, focusing instead on evaluating **QuickBooks** as a simpler alternative (00:21).
- Issues with retainage processing in Acumatica have caused ongoing headaches, confirmed by emails from Jesse.
actionItems: Coordinate with the accounting team to develop a detailed migration plan and data handling approach for transitioning from Acumatica to QuickBooks (11:44)
Identify and select specific test jobs to pilot the QuickBooks integration using a separate test QuickBooks account (10:36)
Follow up with the Job Planner team to review the QuickBooks test setup and resolve any integration issues identified during the trial phase (17:19)
Support the setup of a separate QuickBooks test account and assist with integrating selected test projects to validate the migration approach (13:25)
Provide guidance on differences in retainage handling and purchase order processing between Acumatica and QuickBooks to facilitate smooth migration (07:00)

💻 Software Proficiency: She is skilled in QuickBooks and Paylocity, with basic Excel and Outlook knowledge.

decisions: ## Notes ## **Candidate Evaluation and Hiring Decision** The meeting focused on assessing **Lauren Rohlfs’ bookkeeping and accounting experience** and deciding on her potential hiring for a remote accounting role.

- **Lauren’s accounting background and readiness for full-time work** were confirmed, highlighting approximately **15-16 years of experience** including tax preparation and bookkeeping for multiple accounts (03:17) - She worked in various roles, including CPA firms and electrical contracting bookkeeping, handling up to **12 accounts** simultaneously with bank reconciliations and credit card transactions.

- **Salary and employment terms** were agreed upon, with Lauren accepting a **1099 independent contractor position** and a **pay range between $15 and $20 per hour**, ideally around **$19 to $20** (11:15) - The team plans to start her near the end of the current week, with the offer contingent on internal approval from CEO Brandon and potential reference checks.
actionItems: Provide feedback and final decision on Lauren Rohlfs's candidacy to HR and CEO after reviewing additional candidate results (18:50)
Manage communication with the temporary staffing service regarding termination of the current temp worker, Jose (19:09)
Inform CEO Brandon about Lauren Rohlfs's interview and positive impression
seek CEO's input on hiring decision (16:02)
Prepare and send an offer letter to Lauren Rohlfs with proposed hourly pay between $19 and $20 upon CEO's approval (16:02)

input.message: Were there any issues from the meetings in the last couple days?

output.rationale: The user wants a current-state summary of issues arising from recent meetings, which maps to latest_status. However, no project is selected and 'the last couple days' is ambiguous without project scope or accessible meeting sources, so a clarifying question is needed before retrieving or summarizing meeting-derived issues.

output.reason: Streaming model tools enabled on the configured AI SDK provider. Vercel AI Gateway is preferred when AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is configured; direct OpenAI requires AI_PROVIDER_PATH=openai or no gateway key. Disable by setting AI_ASSISTANT_DISABLE_STREAMING_MODEL_TOOLS=true.

05:35) Flooring crew to start demo, grinding, and sealing in offices, restrooms, break room Painting and carpentry to frame HVAC returns and build cash wrap knee wall Coordination to swap cash stands for new structures Plumbing installations pending flooring and painting completion Toilet, sink, and water fountain rough-ins scheduled HVAC return installation costs reduced and approved Electrical panel relocation paused pending budget and bids Demo and infill door/window to offices completed HVAC team coordinating raising thermostats above gondolas 🏗️ **Canton Site Activities** (05:41 - 07:46) Substantial completion scheduled for June 30 Installing structural angle iron in stock room Painting cell floor and walls Investigating roof leaks with landlord Pending approval for demo of wall soffit supports Facilities staff installing car corrals at Canton and Pioneer 🤝 **Communication and Coordination** (09:01 - 11:15) Praise for collaboration between Alleato’s Nick Brimie and Goodwill’s Andrew Mulder Internal IT shifts may require new contractor selection for office moves Plan to move one office at a time to maintain computer access Backup IT contact identified Addition of Pekin and University stores planned as back-end walkthroughs Walkthrough coordination planned with Jared and Nick 📋 **Noblesville Excel Center Turnover** (12:01 - 14:50) Punchlist and turnover coordination underway Early site inspections and contractor corrections planned for early June Coordination with Chad to oversee process Consideration for limited staff training on equipment like thermostats 👷 **Intern Involvement** (16:15 - 18:34) Facility superintendent to log work orders Andrew Mulder to be notified before Alleato team involvement Centralized management of warranty calls preferred ## Notes ## **Project Progress and Site Updates** The meeting detailed steady progress across multiple site locations with clear next steps and timelines.

- **HVAC return location costs were reviewed and reduced by adjusting return heights, approved by leadership** (04:12) - The cost-saving approach improves budget adherence without compromising functionality - All three subcontractors involved submitted pricing that informed the decision - **Electrical panel relocation decisions are paused pending budget review after incorporating upcoming store phases** (04:34) - Andrew Mulder will review overall budget after receiving estimates from the last four stores - Nick is actively managing site visits and pricing from subcontractors to support this assessment - **Pending approval of front soffit wall demolition costs expected shortly to proceed with structural changes** (06:44) - Allied’s proposal is under review and should be approved the same day as per Andrew - Approval enables timely carpentry work critical to next construction phases - **Landlord involvement in roof repairs reflects ongoing vendor coordination to resolve legacy maintenance issues** (07:02) - This external responsibility requires close communication to align on timelines and patching work - Nick will coordinate taping completion post-repair to finalize soffit area restoration ## **Operational and IT Coordination** The team emphasized the importance of synchronized office moves and IT support to minimize operational disruption.

- **Office moves at Washington will proceed with register swaps and phased IT support to maintain system access** (09:26) - Andrew confirmed approval of register rearrangements to create space for knee walls - Ian, the contracted IT specialist, is moving away, prompting a search for a new contact - Interim support may come from Dan Rit, with Andrew providing his contact for smooth communication - **Moving office equipment one room at a time helps maintain continuous computer access during transitions** (10:13) - This phased approach reduces risk of downtime or lost connectivity among store staff - Open communication channels between site and IT teams are critical for issue resolution - **Interns Victor and Hunter have started supporting site operations and will be introduced to site walkthroughs** (16:18) - They will gain hands-on experience under Douglas Franklin’s supervision at Allisonville and Noblesville - Their presence adds manpower to assist Nick and improve oversight across multiple projects ## **Project Closeout and Turnover Planning** Preparation for project turnovers includes detailed punch list management and staff training considerations.

## Notes ## **Materials Procurement and Budget Management** The team decided to limit the mechanical tees order due to high cost concerns and credit card limits.

- **Vertical pipes painting nearing completion; air compression testing imminent** at UNICO site (00:12) - TJ Graves reported one row of verticals left to paint before testing - The air compressor trailer just arrived, enabling air pressure tests to proceed - This milestone signals readiness to move into the punch list phase soon - **Riser work planned to start tomorrow barring leak repairs** with schedule aligned for next site moves (00:13) - TJ expects to begin riser installation the following day assuming leaks are fixed - Leak fixes are the only visible blocker ahead of riser work start - After finishing here, crews plan to move to GPC site by Thursday or Friday because of delays at CAA site - This sequencing aligns with material availability and racking delays, optimizing workforce use - **Pipe guards relocated to Siva site causing shortfall on current site** requiring retrieval (00:15) - TJ found that pipe guards were moved by Mike to Siva without prior notice - Kebba confirmed enough guards were ordered for both sites but retrieval from Siva is needed - The plan is to stop by Siva and bring the guards back to complete work on current site - This material movement caused a temporary shortage affecting installation progress - **Replacement vertical piece prepared and ready for lab delivery** with clear handoff plan (00:31) - TJ confirmed a replacement vertical pipe is ready to be sent out once approved - Kebba and TJ identified Parker as the likely person to deliver the sample to the lab - Documentation including the job start form with circled pipe locations has been provided to Parker - This ensures lab testing can proceed smoothly with proper traceability ## **Expense Coding and Financial Process Improvements** The team identified common credit card coding errors and agreed on a weekly review process to improve accuracy.

- **Limited mechanical tees order to 10 units instead of 24** to manage the $2,000+ invoice and credit card constraints (00:07) - The original list included 24 tees, but only 10 will be purchased to reduce expense - Greg Davis explained the mechanical tees and couplings are needed for the riser installation - The mechanical tees are expected to arrive after 12 PM and may be picked up later by Parker or others - Cost per mechanical tee is about $33, making the total significant and prompting the cautious order size - **Credit card payment and limit increase process clarified** to avoid maxing out company cards (00:08) - Greg normally uses his card but it’s nearing its limit, causing concern - Kebba Mass explained credit card increases must be requested by Brandon or Maria by phone, not just via app - The team aims to avoid unnecessary payments on credit cards to control spending and avoid maxing out - Parker’s card was authorized for payment of $17+ to cover the current purchase, easing immediate cash flow - **Return and credit for unused supplies discussed** to recover costs on unused materials (00:34) - TJ Graves mentioned $460 spent on Windustrial fittings that were mostly unused due to project shifts - Kebba advised to return unused materials with receipts and note credits for proper accounting - This process helps reduce excess costs and maintain budget discipline - **Credit application submitted to negotiate better prices** for future purchases at Windustrial (00:33) - Greg noted that completing the credit application reduces prices for subsequent orders - Kebba committed to filling out and sending the application to secure lower rates - This strategic step supports cost savings on future material procurement ## **Project Execution and Site Updates** The team is progressing toward testing and final touches with clear plans for riser work and site transitions.

## Shorthand Bullet 📧 **Communication Protocols** (01:00 - 01:30) Emphasis on copying senior PMs and upper management on project correspondence to keep them informed and leverage their knowledge for decision-making.

- **Adding five new team members to cover high-value projects** including Tony (PM starting Monday), Demetrius (assistant PM), Patrick Antonella (project engineer), and two interns, Victor and Hunter, who will assist Nick Bremmy and estimating respectively (08:30) - Tony will focus on the Exo and Vermillion projects, both valued near **$60 million** combined - Patrick has prior experience at FA Wilhelm, expected to bring valuable expertise, especially on large projects like XL Wilmer - Intern Victor supports Illinois projects, while Hunter helps in estimating to alleviate workload - Jesse emphasized the importance of quickly integrating these hires to maintain momentum on multiple concurrent projects - **Recruiting and manpower scheduling improvements underway** with Jasmine managing a dynamic spreadsheet to track project start and stop dates and manpower needs (14:50) - Kebba confirmed current coverage for ongoing projects including Utah and Florida, with potential hiring if new projects arise - Jesse stressed keeping updated manpower plans to avoid last-minute scrambling and overhiring risks - This system aims to balance staffing against workload to avoid firing or underutilization - **Experienced hires bring diverse backgrounds to strengthen team** with Tony’s GC business contacts expected to open new business opportunities in Indianapolis (07:30) - Jesse highlighted Tony’s semi-retired status but strong network as a strategic asset to convert leads into work - Patrick’s transition from teaching to construction management adds valuable project oversight skills for complex builds - **Team morale and office culture remain positive despite growth** with light-hearted banter and recognition of busy schedules, reflecting good camaraderie as team size grows from **4 to over 20** in recent years (05:50) - Jesse noted the pace and scale increase from **$10 million to $28 million** in revenue alongside team expansion - Andrew and Candon exchanged friendly comments, reinforcing a supportive work environment ## **Financial Processes and Cost Control** Improving credit card transaction oversight and cost coding accuracy is a priority to avoid billing delays and budget overruns.

- **Current credit card transaction process causes billing delays and reporting roadblocks** as receipts often lack proper job codes or cost categories, making monthly reporting impossible until resolved (01:50) - Jesse plans to create instructional videos for new staff on correct coding procedures to reduce errors - Ashley will take responsibility for reviewing and verifying transactions to speed up approvals - The team acknowledged this task is critical to close out jobs and complete monthly financial reports - **PMs to increase involvement in weekly/monthly transaction reviews** to better control spending and coding accuracy, potentially during Friday weekly report meetings (18:30) - Kebba suggested a weekly review to catch common mistakes and improve consistency - Jesse emphasized the need for PMs to work closely with supers on coding before final approval to prevent errors and save time - **System visibility and permissions for transaction management explained** with Kebba showing dashboard views and filtering options to monitor all employee expenses (19:50) - Jesse demonstrated the ability to identify incorrect coding, missing receipts, and timing issues on charges - Kebba confirmed PMs can edit and correct cost coding to reduce administrative burden on Jesse - The goal is to empower field leadership to catch mistakes early, reducing the volume of required rework - **Awareness raised on spending habits and vendor costs** with Gregory investigating overcharges on car rentals to ensure compliance with quoted rates (17:30) - Jesse reminded the team to seek bargains and maintain cost-consciousness despite having spending freedoms - Monitoring and controlling discretionary costs is necessary to protect project budgets ## **Project Pipeline and Workload Management** The team is managing a robust pipeline of sprinkler projects and other builds with detailed scheduling and manpower alignment.

- **Copying senior PMs Doug and Bob on key emails is mandated to leverage their experience and maintain alignment** (00:55) - Jesse urged everyone to keep upper management informed on decisions and issues to benefit from their knowledge - This helps avoid miscommunication and keeps leadership in the loop for critical matters - **Monthly billing summaries to be shared with Andrew and Bryce to improve project financial oversight** (02:10) - Doug may review these summaries monthly with Andrew and Bryce to ensure transparency on project build values - This is intended to keep all stakeholders aware of revenue progress and job status - **Jesse plans to schedule next week’s meetings with a split focus between Doug’s and Bob’s teams** to streamline coordination across offices (00:55) - This will help reduce confusion for those working across both groups - Emphasizes clearer lines of communication and responsibility - **Team members encouraged to report manpower schedules and project statuses regularly** to help recruiting and PMs anticipate staffing needs (15:30) - Jesse emphasized using Teams messages or calls to update Jasmine’s spreadsheet promptly - This continuous communication loop supports proactive hiring and resource allocation ## **Productivity Culture and Workload Insights** The team is maintaining strong productivity with high workloads balanced by strategic hiring and process improvements.

- **90% of pipe built for next aisle at Siva site with readiness to install upon permit approval** (13:11) - Racking and pipe assembly are nearly complete, awaiting work permit expected by next weekend - TJ Graves noted the ability to shift crews to GPC site to maintain pace with racking team - Weekend work with 3-4 personnel is planned to keep schedule on track - This flexibility helps mitigate potential permit delays and resource constraints - **Air pressure testing ongoing at Uniqlo, pending equipment delivery from Equipment Share** (06:50) - Compressor was onsite for systems A and B and system 3 air testing - Parker Hollingsworth supervising punch list and pipe painting with one worker assigned to A line guards - Equipment Share invoicing and account setup discussed to streamline billing and payments - Prompt setup of vendor accounts and W9 collection prioritized for smooth invoicing - **GPC site making steady progress with branch pipe installation and air leak fixes on couplings** (06:17) - No holes detected yet; focus on replacing leaking couplings to maintain system integrity - Procurement for missing six-inch couplings and tees expedited through One Industrial to meet next-day delivery needs - Decision to bypass usual supplier Akifier due to lead time constraints illustrates agile supply chain management - **Racking drawings for Exalt site partially available; structural and reaction data pending full sets** (23:40) - Symbotic call updates revealed ongoing revisions incorporating electric overlays and data drops - Use of Revit files creates compatibility challenges for some team members, requiring technical support - Maintaining clarity on documentation status is critical for coordination and scheduling ## **Material Handling and Quality Assurance** Material inspection and pipe condition assessments emphasize thorough documentation and strategic replacement to satisfy stakeholders.
actionItems: Add Equipment Share to the project system and obtain their W9 form to set up billing and invoicing processes for air compressor rental costs (07:42)
Arrange procurement account setup with Wind Industrial to enable faster ordering and procurement of materials (08:48)
Initiate creation of a racking schedule/tracker file for McLean and new projects to document weekly progress and maintain organization (28:45)
Order six-inch pipe couplings, cones, and mechanical tees and arrange for next-day pickup from One Industrial due to urgent timing needs (05:22)
Monitor GPC site for any air leaks during branch line installation and report immediately to coordinate repairs if leaks are detected (06:36)
criticalRisks: Air pressure testing is ongoing at Uniqlo, with equipment delivery anticipated, while the GPC site is addressing pipe leaks and expediting procurement for critical parts.
System A partially completed with gap due to flu pipe delay.
## Notes ## **Project Progress and Site Updates** The teams are steadily advancing on pipe rack installations and system air testing despite some delays caused by missing components.

decisions: ## Shorthand Bullet 👋 **Meeting Start and Attendance** (00:00 - 01:00) Team attendance confirmed.
Mechanical yard location confirmed with screening from US 42.

- **Concerns about 15-17% slope on site impacting maintenance and lawn care** with mobile slope classification confirmed for some areas (29:30) - Steep slopes may challenge mowing and upkeep with tractors or implements.
actionItems: Provide solar panel roofing system design information to Nick Vrettos, Jerome Daksiewicz, and Jim Burke to understand electrical sizing impact and jurisdictional coordination (58:30)
Confirm with Westfield General Managers about rooftop unit and hot water heater performance to identify any operational issues (57:40)
Push for final plan clarifications and layout confirmations with the owner to meet the May 22 deadline for furniture and kitchen equipment layouts (01:10:40)
Coordinate with Douglas Franklin to communicate owner updates promptly to architecture and engineering teams to avoid delays (01:20:35)
Develop and share refined site design including building rotation and entry point adjustments addressing grading and detention pond concerns (24:30)
criticalRisks: The project timeline was established, aiming for a permit submission by late June, alongside a strategy for kitchen equipment planning and electrical distribution.
Furniture layout decisions critical for electrical, mechanical, and lighting designs.
## **Project Timeline and Milestones** A revised project schedule sets clear deadlines for owner approvals and design development, aiming for permit submission by late June.

provider_decision.reason: Streaming model tools enabled on the configured AI SDK provider. Vercel AI Gateway is preferred when AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is configured; direct OpenAI requires AI_PROVIDER_PATH=openai or no gateway key. Disable by setting AI_ASSISTANT_DISABLE_STREAMING_MODEL_TOOLS=true.
